title: Dativverben
summary: Verbs that always take a dative object — helfen, danken, gefallen, and more — a classic trap for English speakers who expect accusative.

sections.heading: Verbs that take Dativ, not Akkusativ
sections.body:
Most German verbs work like their English equivalents: the person or thing affected by the action is a direct object in the accusative case (Ich sehe den Mann. — I see the man). But a specific group of verbs always takes the dative case for that object instead — even though the English sentence looks exactly like a normal transitive verb with a direct object.
This is one of the most common traps for English speakers. "I help him" looks transitive, so learners default to the accusative "ihn" — but German requires the dative: Ich helfe ihm. These verbs aren't secretly two-object verbs like geben (to give someone something); they take only ONE object, and that object is dative, not accusative.
There's no single logical reason these particular verbs take dative instead of accusative — it has to be memorized per verb. One loose pattern that helps: many of them describe how someone relates or responds to another person (helping, thanking, trusting, answering, following) rather than an action performed directly on them.

sections.heading: The most common dative verbs
sections.body: These verbs show up constantly in everyday German. Learn each one as a fixed pair — verb + Dativ — rather than translating word for word from English.

rows: "Helfen" affects the person only indirectly — you assist them, you don't act directly ON them.
Thanking is a response directed at someone, not an action performed on them.
Literally "it is pleasing TO me" — the person is the receiver of the pleasing effect, not its target.
Same pattern as gefallen: food "tastes good TO someone" — an effect experienced by the dative person.
Clothing "fits FOR someone" — again an effect on the dative person, not a direct action.
Belonging describes a relationship TO an owner, not an action done to them.
Following happens in relation to someone, not directly to them (and takes sein in the Perfekt: Ich bin dir gefolgt).
"glauben + Dativ" (person) means believing someone; "glauben an + Akkusativ" means believing IN something — a different construction.
A response directed TO someone; contrast with "antworten auf + Akk" (to respond to something).
Congratulating is directed at someone, not performed on them.
Trust is placed IN someone (a relationship), not an action done to them.
Listening is oriented TOWARD someone; the separable prefix zu- reinforces the direction.
Contradicting responds TO what someone said, not acting directly on the person.

sections.heading: Don't mix them up with accusative verbs
sections.body:
Many everyday verbs that also describe interacting with a person DO take the accusative, which is exactly why dative verbs are such a trap: fragen (to ask), sehen (to see), kennen (to know), lieben (to love), besuchen (to visit), and treffen (to meet) all take a normal accusative object.
Compare: Ich frage ihn. (accusative — I ask him) vs. Ich antworte ihm. (dative — I answer him). Two very similar-sounding actions, two different cases — there's no shortcut except learning each verb's case individually.

exercises.explanation: "schmecken" governs the dative. "die Gäste" (plural) → dative "den Gästen".
"glauben" + Dativ (person) = to believe someone. "ihm" is the dative form of "er".
"gehören" governs the dative. "mein Bruder" (masculine) → dative "meinem Bruder".

exercises.prompt: Sie folgt ___ Anweisungen des Arztes genau.
exercises.explanation: "folgen" governs the dative. "die Anweisungen" (plural) → dative "den Anweisungen".

exercises.prompt: Ich helfe ___ Mann mit den Taschen.
exercises.explanation: "helfen" governs the dative. "der Mann" is masculine, so its dative form is "dem Mann".
"helfen" governs the dative. "die Mutter" is feminine, so its dative form is "der Mutter".
"helfen" governs the dative. "das Kind" is neuter, so its dative form is "dem Kind".
"helfen" governs the dative. Plural nouns take "den" in the dative: "den Eltern".

exercises.prompt: Wir danken ___ Lehrer für die Hilfe.
exercises.explanation: "danken" governs the dative. "der Lehrer" is masculine, so its dative form is "dem Lehrer".

exercises.prompt: Ich danke ___ Ärztin für den guten Rat.
exercises.explanation: "danken" governs the dative. "die Ärztin" is feminine, so its dative form is "der Ärztin".

exercises.prompt: Wir danken ___ Team für die Unterstützung.
exercises.explanation: "danken" governs the dative. "das Team" is neuter, so its dative form is "dem Team".
"gefallen" governs the dative. Plural nouns take "den" in the dative: "den Kindern".
"gefallen" governs the dative. "die Frau" is feminine, so its dative form is "der Frau".
"gefallen" governs the dative. "das Mädchen" is neuter, so its dative form is "dem Mädchen".

exercises.prompt: Die Suppe schmeckt ___ Gast sehr gut.
exercises.explanation: "schmecken" governs the dative. "der Gast" is masculine, so its dative form is "dem Gast".
"schmecken" governs the dative. Plural nouns take "den" in the dative: "den Kindern".
"schmecken" governs the dative. "das Baby" is neuter, so its dative form is "dem Baby".

exercises.prompt: Die Schuhe passen ___ Mann leider nicht.
exercises.explanation: "passen" governs the dative. "der Mann" is masculine, so its dative form is "dem Mann".

exercises.prompt: Die Farbe passt ___ Frau richtig gut.
exercises.explanation: "passen" governs the dative. "die Frau" is feminine, so its dative form is "der Frau".
"passen" governs the dative. "das Kind" is neuter, so its dative form is "dem Kind".
"gehören" governs the dative. "der Chef" is masculine, so its dative form is "dem Chef".
"gehören" governs the dative. "die Lehrerin" is feminine, so its dative form is "der Lehrerin".
"gehören" governs the dative. "die beiden Nachbarn" (plural, "both neighbors") takes "den" in the dative: "den beiden Nachbarn".
"folgen" governs the dative. "die Frau" is feminine, so its dative form is "der Frau".
"folgen" governs the dative. "der Lehrer" is masculine, so its dative form is "dem Lehrer".
"glauben" + Dativ (person) governs the dative. "das Kind" is neuter, so its dative form is "dem Kind".
"antworten" governs the dative. "die Lehrerin" is feminine, so its dative form is "der Lehrerin".
"gratulieren" governs the dative. "die Kolleginnen" (plural) takes "den" in the dative: "den Kolleginnen".
"helfen" governs the dative. The 1st-person dative pronoun is "mir", not the accusative "mich".
"danken" governs the dative. The 2nd-person singular dative pronoun is "dir", not the accusative "dich".

exercises.prompt: Ich glaube ___ nicht, wenn sie das sagt. (wem?)
exercises.explanation: "glauben" + Dativ (person). "sie" (she) becomes the dative pronoun "ihr", not the accusative "sie".
"antworten" governs the dative. The 1st-person plural dative pronoun is "uns" (same form as accusative here).
"gratulieren" governs the dative. The 2nd-person plural dative pronoun is "euch" (same form as accusative here).
"vertrauen" governs the dative. "er" (he) becomes the dative pronoun "ihm", not the accusative "ihn".
"widersprechen" governs the dative. "sie" (they) becomes the dative pronoun "ihnen", not the accusative "sie".

exercises.prompt: Hör ___ gut zu, wenn ich rede! (wem?)
exercises.explanation: "zuhören" governs the dative. The 1st-person dative pronoun is "mir", not the accusative "mich".

exercises.prompt: Der Kellner folgt ___ zum Tisch. (wem? — den Gästen)
exercises.explanation: "folgen" governs the dative. "sie" (they, referring to "die Gäste") becomes the dative pronoun "ihnen".

exercises.prompt: Das Geschenk gefällt ___ sehr. (wem? — dem Vater)
exercises.explanation: "gefallen" governs the dative. "er" (he, referring to "der Vater") becomes the dative pronoun "ihm".

exercises.prompt: Put the words in the correct order.
exercises.explanation: Subject (Ich) + verb (helfe) + dative object (meiner Mutter, feminine dative after "mein" = "meiner") + time expression (jeden Tag). The time expression can also be fronted for emphasis, with subject-verb inversion.

exercises.prompt: Put the words in the correct order.
exercises.explanation: Subject (Der Lehrer) + verb (antwortet) + dative object (den Schülern, plural dative) + adverb (geduldig). The dative object can also be fronted for emphasis, with subject-verb inversion.

exercises.prompt: Put the words in the correct order.
exercises.explanation: Subject (Wir) + verb (gratulieren) + dative object (unserem Freund, masculine dative after "unser" = "unserem") + prepositional phrase (zum Geburtstag). The prepositional phrase can also be fronted for emphasis, with subject-verb inversion.
"helfen" governs the dative, not the accusative. "meine Schwester" (accusative/nominative) must become "meiner Schwester" (feminine dative).
"gefallen" governs the dative. The accusative pronoun "mich" must become the dative pronoun "mir".

exercises.prompt: Wir danken den Lehrer für seine Geduld.
exercises.answer: Wir danken dem Lehrer für seine Geduld.
exercises.explanation: "danken" governs the dative. "der Lehrer" (masculine) takes the dative article "dem", not the accusative "den".
